coredata.dc:title: The “ON-OFF” microwave reaction time technique: A novel strategy to impact the fluorescence of multi-color emissive carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Karim, Marwa
Shokry, Azza
Ebrahim, Shaker
Khalil, Marwa
coredata.dc:description: Multi-colored fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized via the microwave-assisted technique using citric acid (CA). To the best of our knowledge, the “ON-OFF” microwave-assisted technique was investigated for synthesis CDs for the first time. The impact of the “ON-OFF” strategy was monitored on the morphological, structural, and optical properties of the CDs. By increasing the reaction time from 10 to20 min, a reduction in the particle size was detected from 4.1 ± 0.6 to 1.6 ± 0.2 nm, respectively using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). A blue shift was detected in the photoluminescence (PL) as a function of the reaction time with optimal fluorescence intensity at 15 min. The quantum yields (QY) were 15.2 %, 22.2 %, and 11.9 % for the reaction time 10, 15, and 20 min, respectively. Three electronic transitions were observed from the UV-Vis spectra, and their corresponding energy band gap (Eg) were calculated using Tauc plot. The first transition was a reaction time-dependent in the range of 5.1–5.8 eV. While the other transitions were reaction time-independent and kept constant at 4.9 and 4.6 eV. A model was hypothesized to correlate the particle size reduction with the reaction time during the formation of the CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Microwave
“ON-OFF” microwave reaction time technique
Photoluminescence

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs ‘‘ON-OFF’’ microwave reaction time technique
Effect of reaction time on the emission properties of CDs
Excitation-dependent PL emission spectra of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of CDs samples at different reaction time.

XRD analysis for CDs at reaction time 10, 15, and 20 min (a) XRD patterns show the hkl planes, (b) d-spacing and FWHM as a function of reaction time, and (c) a baseline correction for the (002) plane.
Photographs of the CDs samples at different reaction time during the dialysis process (a) t = 10 min, (b) t = 15 min, and (c) t = 20 min.

TEM micrographs (scale bar: 50 nm) of CDs at different reaction time (a) 10 min, (b) 15 min, and (c) 20 min. Inset of Fig. 5(c) shows HRTEM micrograph (scale bar: 20 nm) for the t = 20 min with the aggregated particles.

HRTEM analysis for CDs at reaction time 10, 15, and 20 min (a), (d), and (g) depict micrographs of CDs with a focus area on the d-spacing, (b), (e), and (h) show particle size distribution histograms, (c), (f), and (i) present the SAED patterns, (j) shows the particle size distribution skewness versus the reaction time, and (k) depicts the average particle size distribution as a function of the reaction time.
UV-Vis spectra of the CDs at reaction time 10, 15, and 20 min (a) the three absorption bands of the CDs, (b) a blue shift in the far UV region at ~200 nm.
UV-Vis spectra of CDs at reaction time 10, 15, and 20 min (a) the absorption edge from the π-π * electron transition band, and (b) absorption edge as a function of reaction time.

Tauc plots of the CDs on the whole spectra at, (a) 10 min, (b) 15 min, (c) 20 min, (d) shows a comparison of the Eg values extracted from the same tangential line on the Tauc plot at different reaction time, and (e) the extracted Eg as a function of reaction time.

PL emission spectra for the CDs at reaction time 10, 15 and 20 min (a) a blue shift in the emission spectra is detected by increasing reaction time, (b) shows the QY graph where the integrated fluorescence intensity (λex = 350 nm) is plotted versus the absorbance of the CDs and the reference dye, (c) a normalized PL spectrum of the same samples, and (d) depicts the FWHM of the PL spectra as a function of the reaction time in a comparison with the standard deviation of the particle size from HRTEM micrographs.
The stacked PL emission spectra are plotted as a function of λex in the range (310–450 nm) for CDs samples at reaction time 10, 15, and 20 min.

The deconvoluted fluorescence spectra from PL emission spectra (λex = 350 nm) for the CDs synthesized at reaction time (a) 10 min, (b)15 min, and (c) 20 min, and (d) shows the four PL states deconvoluted from each PL emission spectra as a function of λex for 10 min CDs sample.
Schematic representation of the electron transition mechanisms in CDs samples.
The calculations from XRD patterns using Gaussian fitting.
List for the d-spacing calculations from the diffraction rings in SAED patterns.

head.ce:title: The “ON-OFF” microwave reaction time technique: A novel strategy to impact the fluorescence of multi-color emissive carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Alexandria University, Moharram Bek, P.O. Box 21511, Alexandria, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Alexandria University

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Materials Science Department, Institute of Graduate Studies and Research, Alexandria University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Science Department, Institute of Graduate Studies and Research, Alexandria University, P.O. Box 832, Alexandria, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology and Composite Materials Department, Institute of New Materials and Advanced Technology, City of Scientific Research and Technological Applications (SRTA-City), New Borg El Arab City, P.O. Box 21934, Alexandria, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanotechnology and Composite Materials Department, Institute of New Materials and Advanced Technology, City of Scientific Research and Technological Applications (SRTA-City)
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: New Borg El Arab City, P.O. Box 21934

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology and Composite Materials Department, Institute of New Materials and Advanced Technology, City of Scientific Research and Technological Applications (SRTA-City), New Borg El Arab City, P.O. Box 21934, Alexandria, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-colored fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized via the microwave-assisted technique using citric acid (CA). To the best of our knowledge, the “ON-OFF” microwave-assisted technique was investigated for synthesis CDs for the first time. The impact of the “ON-OFF” strategy was monitored on the morphological, structural, and optical properties of the CDs. By increasing the reaction time from 10 to20 min, a reduction in the particle size was detected from 4.1 ± 0.6 to 1.6 ± 0.2 nm, respectively using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). A blue shift was detected in the photoluminescence (PL) as a function of the reaction time with optimal fluorescence intensity at 15 min. The quantum yields (QY) were 15.2 %, 22.2 %, and 11.9 % for the reaction time 10, 15, and 20 min, respectively. Three electronic transitions were observed from the UV-Vis spectra, and their corresponding energy band gap (Eg) were calculated using Tauc plot. The first transition was a reaction time-dependent in the range of 5.1–5.8 eV. While the other transitions were reaction time-independent and kept constant at 4.9 and 4.6 eV. A model was hypothesized to correlate the particle size reduction with the reaction time during the formation of the CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, CDs have grabbed tremendous attention as a new member of the carbon family that have outstanding advantages such as low production cost, abundant raw materials [1], good biocompatibility [2,3], and superior photoluminescent properties (e.g., high photostability and excitation-dependent fluorescence emission) [4]. Accordingly, intensive research has been dedicated to advance their unique photoresponses [5].

To fully expand CDs in wide applications, it is recommended to prepare them with characteristic features such as high QY [6–8], long-wavelength emission [8,9], and dual or multi-colored emission centers. Successfully, a breakthrough was achieved through the recent discovery of their dual/multi-colored emissions behavior (i.e., display two, or more fluorescence emissions under one-wavelength excitation). Such property enables them to be integrated with optically related applications such as bioimaging, biolabeling, sensors [10,11], fingerprint detections [12], and solid-state lighting devices [13–15].

To deploy CDs in the above-mentioned applications, it is essential for them to exhibit a fluorescence from blue to red. Consequently, the rapid development in multi-colored emissions CDs materials and synthesis techniques is highly recommended. Also, it has been reported that the luminescence mechanisms are attributed to core-related emission [16–18], surface state-related emission [10,19], electronic conjugate structures, and free zig-zag sites [20]. However, the luminescence mechanism of CDs is still an open question. Hence, the deeper understanding of the origin of fluorescence in CDs, the easier controlling their emissions.

CDs have been prepared by several techniques, specifically, hydrothermal synthesis [5,21], ultrasound [22], electrochemical etching [23–25], and microwave-assisted technique [26,27]. However, the microwave-assisted technique has grabbed attention due to their advantages such as shorter reaction time (i.e., environmentally friendly, energy-saving, and lower costs), high reproducibility, and uniform distribution of energy inside the reaction vessel (i.e., volume heating).

Herein, multi-color fluorescence CDs are prepared via a one-step microwave-assisted approach using only CA in an aqueous solution. Through the presented work, the relatively new “ON-OFF” microwave reaction time strategy is introduced to alert the formation mechanisms of CDs. The morphological, structural, and optical characteristics of the synthesized CDs are deeply investigated. Moreover, a proposed new model to correlate the formation mechanism of CDs, is proposed. Our work is a step toward finding effective ways to optimize and tune the fluorescence of CDs based on altering the synthesis parameters.

Citric Acid (CA) anhydrous (Purity grade of 99.9 %) was purchased from (LOBA Chemie, India), which was used as received without any further purification. The de-ionized water (DIW) was used throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs ‘‘ON-OFF’’ microwave reaction time technique
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs samples were synthesized through a microwave-assisted procedure, which is proposed by Qu et al. [28]. Briefly, 1.01 g of CA was added to 10 mL of DIW to form a transparent solution. Then, the solution was vigorously stirred for 30 min at room temperature to homogenize the mixture. Then, the solution was subjected to microwave irradiation in a microwave oven (output power: 900 W, 2450 MHz) for the reaction time of 10, 15, and 20 min using the “ON-OFF” microwave reaction time technique.

In the “ON-OFF” microwave reaction time technique, the CA solution was subjected to microwave irradiation (i.e., “ON” timing) and then the irradiation was stopped during the experiment (i.e., “OFF” timing). The 10 min sample was continuously irradiated for 10 min in a step of 0 min OFF. The 15 min sample was adjusted in a step of 0.5 min OFF between each 1 min irradiation. Similarly, the 20 min sample was settled in a step 1 min OFF between each 1 min irradiation. Thus, the total irradiation time exposed for these samples was kept fixed at 10 min. During the heat treatment in the microwave oven, the transparent solution was firstly carbonized and then turned into a brown solution. After the complete vaporization of water, a dark-brown clustered solid was formed, which indicates the formation of the CDs. The brown solid mass was kept cooling down naturally at room temperature for the subsequent purification process.

Once the CDs were completely cooled down to room temperature, the CDs were dispersed in DIW via ultrasonication, and then purified by using centrifuging (6500 rpm, 30 min) to remove any insoluble, large, or agglomerated particles, as shown in Fig. 4. Then, the yellow supernatant was extracted and subjected to the subsequent dialysis process. In such a process, the supernatant was placed inside (FREY scientific, USA) dialysis tubing membrane with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 1000 Dalton against DIW for several days.

To perform the QY experiment by the slope method, five different concentrations were prepared for all samples: the 10-, 15-, and 20-min CDs samples. Also, five different concentrations of the quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 solution, fluorescent QY = 54 %) were used as the reference dye [29]. Each concentration belong to certain sample was subjected to the UV-Vis and the absorbance was recorded below 0.1 at λex= 350 nm to minimize reabsorption. Afterwards, all the concentrations were tested using PL at λex= 350 nm. The integrated fluorescence intensity was calculated from the PL and plotted versus the absorbance for each sample. Finally, the QY for the CDs samples and the reference dye was calculated using the relative approach via the following equation (Eq. (1)) [30]:
(1)
Q
X
=
Q
ST
(
Grad
X
Grad
ST
)
(
η
X
2
η
ST
2
)
where Q is the fluorescence QY, Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance curve, and η is the refractive index of solvents. The subscript “X” refers to the CDs samples (i.e., the 10-, 15-, and 20-min CDs samples) and “ST” refers to the reference dye.

HRTEM micrographs of the CDs were captured by a (JEOL, Model JEM-2100 LaB6, Japan) microscope system operating. For that purpose, the aqueous CDs were dispersed in ethanol and sonicated, and then a drop of the solution was dripped onto ultra-thin copper grids. HRTEM is an essential tool to determine the morphology, the particle size distribution, and the interplanar lattice spacing of the CDs. Furthermore, it is used to investigate the crystal structure/amorphous nature of the CDs via the selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) patterns.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected from (Bruker, Model D2 PHASER, German) diffractometer, operating at 30 kV and 10 mA. It is equipped with an X-ray source with a monochromatic copper filter (Cu-Ka radiation, λ = 0.154184 nm), which is used as the radiation source and the (LYNXEYE, Si-strip) is the detector. The pattern was recorded in the diffraction angle (2θ) range of 0–100° at room temperature, with a step interval of 0.01° and an integration time of 0.1 s at each step. The samples for XRD were fabricated by drop-casting on a carefully ultrasonic-cleaned and hydrophilic-treated glass substrate (1 × 1 cm2). XRD is a fundamental tool to determine the-d‐spacing of the interplanar planes, and the crystalline structure/the amorphous nature of the CDs.

The Ultraviolet (UV-Vis) absorption spectra of the CDs were recorded at room temperature in the spectrum range 190–700 nm with a step interval of 2 nm using a UV-Vis spectrometer (Evolution 300, Model Thermo Scientific, USA). All aqueous CDs were diluted in DIW and placed in quartz cuvettes (1.0 cm pathlength). UV-Vis is a basic tool to obtain the optical properties and the electronic transitions between the valence and conduction bands of the CDs. The optical Eg of electrons transition between orbitals was extracted through the Tauc-plots extracted from the UV-Vis spectra.

The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of the CDs in isopropanol were determined via (Spectrum BX 11- LX 18–5255 Perkin Elmer, German). The spectra were collected in the transmission mode with the wavenumber range of 350–4000 cm−1 at 2.0 cm resolution. FTIR is used to qualify the surface state of the CDs, identify the chemical bonding configuration, and the surface structure.

The down-conversion PL emission spectra of the CDs in an aqueous solution were carried out at room temperature with a (Perkin Elmer, Model LS-55, German) fluorescence spectrometer. A xenon lamp is used as the excitation source with a single grating monochromator, and a photomultiplier tube is used as the detector. The excitation and emission slits were adjusted at 10 and 10 nm, respectively. All aqueous CDs were diluted in DIW and placed in quartz cuvettes (1.0 cm pathlength).

The FTIR absorption spectra for the CDs are presented in Fig. 1. The stretching vibration of the O-H (hydroxyl) group results in broadband from 3620 to 3050 cm−1 and centered at 3340 cm−1. Such broadening indicates that the O-H functional group participates in several hydrogen bonding between the CDs. While the peak at 1375 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of the O-H group. The peaks around 2970, 2884, and 816 cm−1 are attributed to asymmetrical and symmetrical axial stretching vibrational mode, and the out-of-plane bending mode of the C-H functional group, respectively. The small absorption peak at 1765 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of the CO (carbonyl) group, which suggested the surface passivation of the CDs by surface groups during the carbonization process of CA. Moreover, the band from 1708 to 1592 cm−1 and center at 1650 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CC (aromatic nuclei). The axial stretching vibration of the C–O (alkoxy) functional group is displayed in the absorption bands 1158 and 1025 cm−1 [31–33].

All CDs samples show no differences in the functional groups adsorbed on the surface, which indicate that there is no abrupt change in the surface state/defects of the CDs by increasing the reaction time. Hence, it is expected that the PL transition mechanisms and the origin of the fluorescence will be the same in all CDs samples. Also, the presence of both the hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties on the surface of the CDs implied their solubility and stability in an aqueous medium [34]. The functionalization of the synthesized CDs with all the detected functional groups reinforces them to be used as linkers with drugs moieties for medical applications.

The structural characterization is also investigated by using XRD. Fig. 2a depicts XRD patterns of CDs at different reaction time. The XRD patterns of CDs samples show a strong peak centered at 2θ of 23.7°, 25.4°, and 26.4° for the reaction time 10, 15, and 20 min, respectively. It is attributed to the (002) plane (JCPDS, Card No. 75-1621), which is one of the basic planes of graphitic carbon (i.e., the (00l) series of the typical hexagonal graphite planes). In addition to the graphite plane, two other shoulders are assigned to the (103) and (101) planes, which belonged to the amorphous carbon.

A gradual increase is observed in the diffraction angles for the (002) peaks by increasing the reaction time. Hence, Bragg’s law is applied to extract the d-spacing values. All calculations are listed in Table 1 and depicted in Fig. 2b. The d-spacing values are higher than the graphitic interlayer spacing (0.33 nm), which suggeststhe poor crystalline nature of CDs. Furthermore, the characteristic hump under the peaks implies the dominant amorphous nature of the synthesized CDs, which is attributed to the highly disordered carbon atoms [35]. Hence, all the diffraction peaks and shoulders in the XRD patterns are supported by the co-existence of both graphitic and amorphous carbon phases in the prepared CDs.

The broadening of the (002) plane suggests the ultrafine particle size of the CDs. To estimate the effect of reaction time on the average particle size, a baseline correction is performed on the (002) peak (see Fig. 2c) to calculate of the full width at half maximum (FWHM). Then, a Gaussian fitting was applied to estimate the FWHM and the area under the curve, which are listed in Table 1 and depicted in Fig. 2c. The obvious gradual increases in the FWHM and the estimated values of the FWHM confirm the reduction in the average particle size of the CDs by increasing the reaction time.

Notably, the reduction in the CDs particle size with the reaction time is observed from the gradual transient in the samples’ colors from light brown to pale yellow (i.e., a direct result of the quantum confinement effect of the CDs), as shown in Fig. 3.

The morphology, the structure, and the particle size distribution of CDs are identified by using HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 4, the HRTEM micrographs exhibit a quasi-spherical and well-dispersed nature of CDs. However, some aggregations of particles are formed at the longer reaction time (20 min) as shown by the marked blue area in Fig. 4c.

A statistical analysis of 40 particles is performed by using ImageJ software on CDs samples. Fig. 5b, e, and h show the particle size distribution histograms, which estimate 3.1–5.2 nm, 2.0–3.1 nm, and 1.1–1.9 nm for the reaction time of 10, 15, and 20 min, respectively. The particle size distribution skewness is deduced from the same statistics. As shown in Fig. 5j, the skewness values convert from positive to lower negative values, and then to higher negative values as the reaction time increases. Hence, it is demonstrated an asymmetrical deviation of the particle size distribution toward ultrafine particles by increasing the reaction time. To confirm the skewness observations, the average particle size is calculated and demonstrated in Fig. 5k. The particle size of the CDs samples is found as follows: 4.1 ± 0.6 nm, 2.6 ± 0.3 nm, and 1.6 ± 0.2 nm for the reaction time of 10, 15, and 20 min, respectively and are depicted in the insets of Fig. 6a, d, and g. It is ofgreat consistency with both the gradual transient in the samples’ colors and the XRD results. In that context, the formation of aggregations at 20 min (see Fig. 5c) is due to the formation of ultrafine particles of the CDs. This aggregation is due to that no passivation process is used.

It worth be noting that the standard deviation is decreased by increasing the reaction time, which is observed by the reduction in the length of the error bar in Fig. 5k. It is indicated that the inhomogeneity in particle size is reduced by increasing the reaction time. A longer reaction time calls for homogeneity and reduction in size but competes with the formation of aggregation.

Finally, the d‐spacing is also deduced from the HRTEM micrographs and depicted in the insets of Fig. 5a, d, and g. The HRTEM insets show clearly the lattice fringes which confirms the graphitic nature of the CDs [36]. The d‐spacings for the (002) plane are 0.37, 0.35, and 0.34 nm for the 10, 15, and 20 min CDs, respectively. Also, thed‐spacings can also be calculated using the SAED patterns, which are presented in Fig. 5c, f, and i. The SAED patterns exhibit the concentric diffraction rings of the corresponding lattice plane for all samples, which are marked and labeled on the patterns.

The calculations of the lattice planes from the SAED patterns are listed in Table 2. Successfully, the values of the (002) lattice plane coincide with those detected from both HRTEM micrographs and XRD patterns. The calculated value for the third-diffraction ring in the SAED pattern (listed in Table 2) is assigned to the (100) plane in graphitic carbon [37], which was also presented by XRD patterns. Finally, the concentric diffraction rings of SAED patterns in samples resemble the amorphous nature of CDs [38], which is similar to the characteristic humps in XRD.

UV–vis spectroscopy is a fundamental tool for analyzing the optical properties of fluorescent CDs. As depicted in Fig. 6a, the CDs samples are composed of three absorption bands in the UV-Vis spectra, which indicate the presence of three transitions in the UV region for the synthesized CDs. The first band is absorbed strongly in the far UV region at ~200 nm, which is attributed to the π-π * electrons transition from aromatic sp2 hybrid orbitals of the CC bonds (i.e., graphitic/carbon core). The two other shoulders absorbed in the UV region at ~ 230 and 290 nm are attributed to the n-π * non-bonding electrons transition from the sp3 hybrid orbitals of the carbonyl group (CO), which passivating the CDs surface (i.e., surface state/defects) as analyzed in FTIR. Finally, a tail extends in the visible region to 700 nm without noticeable structures [18,39].

Fig. 6b demonstrated a blue shift in the absorption bands values for the π-π * electron transition by increasing the reaction time. Thus, the absorption edge values are extracted as shown in Fig. 7a. It is observed that the absorption edge values are decreased as the reaction time increases, as displayed in Fig. 7b. The blue shift is crucial evidence of the reduction in the particle size of CDs with the reaction time, which is consistent with the XRD and HRTEM analysis.

To investigate the electronic structure and electronic transitions between the HOMO-LUMO states of the CDs, Eg is calculated from the Tauc relation by the following equation (Eq. (2)) [40–43]:
(2)
α
h
ν
=
B
(
h
ν
−
E
g
)
n
where: hν is the photon energy, B is the band tailing parameter (proportional factor), Eg is the optical energy band gap, n = (½ or 2) for direct and indirect band gap respectively, and α is the molar absorption coefficient. Notably, the absorption coefficient (α) at the corresponding wavelengths, is calculated from Beer-Lambert's relation as in the following equation (Eq. (3)) [44]:
(3)
α =
2.303
A
l
where: A is the absorbance and l is the path length. Hence, the optical Eg is extracted from the intercept of the tangent line of the (αhν)2 versus (hν) plot (i.e., Tauc plots) as shown in Fig. 8.

It is observed that the three Eg values are (5.3, 4.9, and 4.6 eV), (5.5, 4.9, and 4.6 eV), and (5.8, 4.9, and 4.6 eV) for the reaction time 10, 15, and 20 min, respectively. They are attributed to three electronic transitions between the HOMO-LUMO states of the CDs, as shown in Fig. 8a–c. A comparison of the fitting data is performed between each reaction time and its corresponding Eg value, as shown in Fig. 8d. Also, the same comparison was performed for the three Eg values at the different reaction time, as depicted in Fig. 8e. Remarkably, one of the Eg is dependent on the reaction time (i.e., Eg increased as the reaction time increased). It is well compatible with the size reduction behavior (i.e., quantum confinement effect). Hence, it could be originated from the energy states in the graphitic core. While the other two Eg are independent on the reaction time, as displayed in Fig. 8e. Thus, they could be originated from the same source, which is related to the surface state (i.e., the hosted defects or functional groups on the surface such as CO functional group).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time on the emission properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To deeply explore the optical properties of CDs, the PL emission spectra are examined versus the different reaction time. As shown in Fig. 9a, the CDs samples are excited at the PL optimal excitation wavelength λex of 350 nm, and the corresponding PL emission spectra are recorded from 330 to 600 nm. The PL optimal emission wavelength is blue-shifted slightly from 455.4 to 443.1 nm by increasing the reaction time from 10 to 20 min as shown in Fig. 9a. The blue shift from PL spectra is another confirmation of the reduction in the average particle size of the CDs.

The fluorescence intensity is increased gradually for the reaction time 10 and 15 min, and then reduces at 20 min sample as displayed in Fig. 9a. Also as shown in Fig. 9b, the QYs of CDs are tested using quinine sulfate (QY = 54 %) as the reference dye and are calculated to be 15.2 %, 22.2 %, and 11.9 % for the reaction time 10, 15, and 20 min, respectively. Thus, the 15 min sample exhibits the optimal reaction time with the highest fluorescent intensity and QY value. To correlate the fluorescence intensity and QY behavior of the CDs with the reaction time, a Gaussian fitting is applied to the normalized PL emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 9(c and d), the FWHM is slightly decreased by increasing the reaction time, which behaves similarly to the standard deviation of the particle size distribution from HRTEM analysis.

As shown in Fig. 9d, the highest FWHM value for the 10 min sample is assigned to the large inhomogeneity in the particle size distribution. Thus, it causes a reduction in the frequency of the formation of emission traps arising from the changes in the hybridization of graphitic cores and π domains on the surface of CDs (i.e., CDs emit at slightly different wavelengths) [45]. Notably, the emissive traps in CDs occurred due to the synergistic effect of both graphitic/carbon core and the surface state/defect conditions. However, the effect of the emissive traps originating from the surface state/defect as a function of the reaction time is ignored (based on UV-Vis and FTIR analysis). Based on the previous explanation, the 15 min sample exhibits the highest QY value and fluorescence intensity due to the reduction in the FWHM and the standard deviation of the particle size distribution, as shown in Fig. 9d. These lower values result in a more homogenous particle size, which in turn boosted the frequency of the formation of emission traps arising from the graphitic/carbon core. However, the 20 min sample presents the lowest QY value and fluorescence intensity with the more reduction in the FWHM and the standard deviation of the particle size distribution as illustrated in Fig. 9d. It is attributed to the aggregation of the ultrafine particle size (1.6 ± 0.2 nm) for the 20 min CDs as observed in the HRTEM micrographs. The aggregated CDs tend to decrease the number of emissive traps and hence impede the fluorescence intensity of CDs for the 20 min CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excitation-dependent PL emission spectra of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate thoroughly the fluorescence property and luminescence mechanisms of CDs, the wavelength of the PL emission spectra is investigated as a function of the PL excitation spectra. Each sample was excited at the wavelength range (310–450 nm), with 20 nm a step. As shown in Fig. 10, the CDs samples display a broad asymmetrical band in the blue-green region with a redshift in the PL optimal emission wavelength in both the near UV and the visible spectra. A multi-Gaussian function deconvolution is performed individually on the whole set of PL spectra as depicted in Fig. 11. It is observed that each PL spectrum is well-fitted by four PL emission peaks (namely, P1 to P4), which are singled out in the (330–600 nm) range of the PL emission spectra. The four PL states are extracted from each PL emission spectra as a function of PL excitation spectra and displayed in Fig. 11d.

It is noted that P1 and P2 exhibit a redshift in the PL emission spectra by increasing excitation wavelength (i.e., excitation-dependent emission spectrum). It is reported that the redshift in the PL emission spectra is a characteristic feature of the radiative recombination that originated due to the quantum confinement of emissive energy traps on the CDs surface dominated by the size effect [16,18]. Also, the redshift behavior of CDs is assigned to the existence of different energy levels, which correspond to the maximum transition probability [24]. Thus, P1 and P2 consider as intrinsic fluorescence and originated from the π-π * electrons transitions between the HOMO-LUMO of the SP2 nanodomain in the graphitic core, which originate from the inhomogeneity in the particle size, as depicted in the schematic of Fig. 12. [46,47].

On the other hand, the P3 and P4 behave differently by increasing the excitation wavelength. Their responses are split into two zones, the high energy zone (below 400 nm) and the low energy zone (above 400 nm), which is marked by the dotted line in Fig. 11d. For the P3, the emission spectra are almost constant for both the high and low energy zones but with a transition to a higher value for the low energy zone (i.e., excitation-independent emission spectrum). The same behavior is observed for the P4 but with higher values for both the high and low energy zones and the transition between them. It is noticed that the fluorescence of the CDs with the excitation-independent emission spectrum is attributed to the surface state defects/surface molecular fluorophores (defects) [10,19]. Thus, the P3 and P4 states are considered as extrinsic fluorescence and emitted from n-π * electronic transitions of two different types of surface emissive traps passivating the CDs as depicted in Fig. 12 [46,47]. Also, the extrinsic emissions such as P3 and P4 can be excited by energy transfer from the graphitic core of CDs (i.e., the UV-blue range excitation) or by direct pumping (i.e., visible range excitation) [48]. Additionally, the transition presented between the HUMO-LUMO of each type of surface defects may result in the energy transient between the different zones.

In a summary, it is obvious that the fluorescence of CDs originated from the formation of electron-hole pair in the band gap state of CDs core (i.e., intrinsic centers) followed by the energy transfer between the HUMO-LOMO of two different surface state defects (i.e., extrinsic surface centers), and energy transitions between the HUMO-LOMO states as schematically represented in Fig. 12 [47,48].

The formation mechanism of the CDs is described by four steps, namely: polymerization, aromatization, nucleation, and growth process [49]. Based on the reported mechanism, the increasing in the irradiation/reaction time results in larger particle size formation. However, our CDs samples exhibit a particle size reduction with a more homogeneity by increasing the reaction time. The reduction in the particle size to the novel “ON-OFF” microwave reaction time technique is assigned.

Simply, the 10 min CDs sample obeys the normal protocol with the continuous microwave irradiation. However, for the 15 min CDs sample, the “OFF” timing is introduced and settled in a step of 0.5 min between each 1 min irradiation. The “OFF” timing causes an abrupt change in the volume heating temperature, which in turn slows down the formation mechanism (i.e., resulted in the cooling/freezing step). Consequently, it is implied a further homogenous nucleation process, which is marked by the better homogeneity in the particle size. Also, it is resulted in a suppression in the growth process, which is announced by the reduction in the particle size. Similarly for the 20 min CDs sample, the “OFF” timing is increased in a step of 1 min between each 1 min irradiation time. Thus, it is slowed down furtherly the timing of the formation mechanisms and implies more homogeneity in particle size and more reduction in the particle size. It is observed that the formation of ultrafine particle size (1.6 ± 0.2 nm) for the 20 min reaction time results in more aggregations on the expenses of the homogeneity and reduction in size. It is assigned to the lacking capping or passivation during the synthesis of the CDs.

A novel modification in the microwave reaction time technique was presented to alter the formation mechanisms of multi-colored emissive CDs. The “ON-OFF” technique resulted in a reduction in the average particle size of CDs as follows: 4.1 ± 0.6 nm for the 10 min, 2.6 ± 0.3 nm for the 15 min, and 1.6 ± 0.2 nm for the 20 min. The “OFF” timing induced an abrupt change in the volume heating temperature and suppressed both the nucleation and growth process (i.e., induced a cooling/freezing process). The suppression in the nucleation process reflected in a better homogeneity for the CDs size. The slowdown in the growth process resulted in a reduction in the average particle size. Consequently, the reduction and homogeneity in the particle size enhanced the optical properties of the CDs with the highest fluorescence intensity and QY value of 22.2 % for reaction time of 15 min. Also, the CDs exhibited a blue shift in the PL emission spectra as a function of reaction time (i.e., a reaction time dependent PL emission spectra). The luminescence mechanisms of CDs were ascribed to intrinsic and extrinsic emissions, namely, the band gap state (graphitic/carbon core), two types of surface state defects, and energy transitions between each the HUMO-LOMO states. Our work is a preliminary step for the preparation of full-color CDs for biosensors and electronic applications based on tunning the synthesis parameters of CDs.

Marwa Karim synthesized the CDs samples, performed PL, calculated all the statistical analysis, contributed to ideas and interpretation of the data, and wrote the manuscript. Azza Shokry shared in the characterization and their analysis. Shaker Ebrahim is the group leader, who set the idea, supervised and designed the work, and reviewed and proofread the manuscript. Marwa Khalil contributed to ideas, experiment execution, and interpretation of the data, and reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129735.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
